title-group.article-title: Association of pruritus with comorbidities and survival in chronic kidney disease: a narrative review of the pre-difelikefalin era literature

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Pruritus is a frequent and distressing symptom in chronic kidney disease (CKD). This study aimed to evaluate the association between pruritus and comorbidities or survival in CKD before the approval of difelikefalin (DFK), a novel treatment for CKD-associated pruritus (CKD-aP). A systematic literature review was conducted using PubMed and Embase, including studies published between January 2000 and August 2022 that examined the link between CKD-aP and morbidity or survival in CKD. We analyzed 5,867 title/abstracts (3,541 from Pubmed, 2,313 from Embase, and nine additional articles) and excluded 5,721 references. Thirty-seven of the 146 articles that were eligible for full reading were included in the analysis. Thus, 18 identified a statistically significant association between CKD-aP and mortality. However, this association lost significance in 3 studies after adjusting for sleep. Two additional studies found no association between CKD-aP and mortality. In terms of sleep disturbance, 14 out of 15 studies reported a significant link with CKD-aP. Moreover, six studies demonstrated relationship between CKD-aP and cardiovascular events, which remained robust even after adjusting for diabetes. The connection between CKD-aP and mental health and depressive symptoms was investigated in 20 studies, with 18 confirming a significant association between the outcomes. Finally, six studies indicated that healthcare costs were higher in CKD-aP compared to those without. Pruritus appears to be associated with increased mortality, cardiovascular events, depressive symptoms, and healthcare costs in CKD patients. Early detection and treatment, particularly with new drugs like DFK, may improve patients’ quality of life and reduce CKD-aP related.

funding-group.funding-statement: This study was not supported by any sponsor or funder.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Chronic pruritus is an unpleasant sensation that leads to the urge to scratch for more than 6 weeks [1,2].

It is a very distressing symptom: the impact on quality of life is similar to that of chronic pain [3,4]. The negative impact of pruritus on sleep quality, work productivity, depression, anxiety, and suicidal ideation have been demonstrated in dermatological or systemic conditions [5,6].

Chronic kidney disease-associated pruritus (CKD-aP), also known as uremic pruritus, has been studied extensively. A meta-analysis of 42 studies showed that pruritus was present in 55% of CKD patients [7]. Another study showed that the presence of pruritus was a risk factor for death after at 3 years in a small cohort of CKD patients [8].

Although pruritus is widely recognized as distressing, its association with comorbidities, disease progression, and survival has been little studied. This narrative review examines these aspects. Before 2021, its therapeutic management remained challenging with various topical and systemic therapies. In 2021, the FDA’s approval and marketing of difelikefalin (DFK), a new parenteral peripheral kappa opioid receptor agonist has changed the game by offering an effective treatment to reduce pruritus and improve patients’ quality of life [9,10]. However, its impact on the many physical and psychological parameters affected in these patients remains to be explored further. So, the aim of this study was to evaluate the association of pruritus with chronic kidney disease (CKD) comorbidities or survival in the literature prior to the DFK. We felt that an exhaustive description of the burden of CKD-aP prior to the DFK era was of paramount importance in order to accurately analyze the effects of this new drug in real life.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We conducted a narrative systematic review following PRISMA 2020 guidelines (Supplementary Figure 1) and approved by the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews, PROSPERO (CRD42022316850).

sec.title: Source and research equation
sec.p: Articles published between January 2000 and August 2022 were retrieved from PubMed and Embase using search terms related to pruritus, mortality, and comorbidities following the equation: (‘pruritus’ or ‘pruritis’ or ‘itch’ or ‘itching’) AND (‘mortality’ or ‘death’ or ‘prognosis’ or ‘prognostic’ or ‘development’ or ‘survival’ or ‘paraneoplasia’ or ‘risk’ or ‘morbidity’ or ‘comorbidity’), NOT (‘psoriasis’ or ‘eczema’ or ‘atopic dermatitis’ or ‘CTCL’ (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma) or ‘Sezary’ or ‘mycosis fungoid’ or ‘prurigo’ or ‘urticaria’ or ‘rash’ or ‘lichen’ or ‘pemphigoid’ or ‘pemphigus’ or ‘scabies’ or ‘lice’ or ‘chickenpox’ or ‘exanthema’).

sec.title: Selection of studies and data extraction
sec.p: Concerning inclusion criteria, only studies with adults (>18 years) were considered. Studies solely published as an abstract were included as long as quantitative information about presence of CKD-aP and its association with the patient outcomes was detailed.
Studies were excluded if they were in languages other than French or English and if they focused on dermatological conditions, animal models, climate-related topics, or pruritus as an adverse effect.

The initial screening was carried out by a single reviewer based on an examination of titles and abstracts, which resulted in an initial selection of eligible articles. In cases of doubt, the full article was analyzed. This list of eligible articles was validated by a second reviewer. In cases of disagreement, a discussion between the two reviewers based on the full-text article, took place in order to reach a consensus.

Data extraction was realized by one reviewer and included study details (authors, year, country), study design, sample size, pruritus intensity and etiology, comorbidities, mortality, sleep, anxiety, depression, and quantitative scales used when mentioned. The second reviewer confirmed and completed the extracted data if necessary.

sec.title: Ethics statement
sec.p: Ethical approval was not required for this systematic review, as the study was based solely on analysis of data from previously published research articles. No new data involving human participants or animals were collected or processed.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Out of 5,867 articles, 37 were included: 27 on hemodialysis (HD), two on peritoneal dialysis (PD), two on non-dialysis CKD, and six on chronic renal failure (Figure 1, Table 1). Studies examined physical comorbidities (n = 16) among them cardiovascular comorbidities (n = 6), mortality (n = 18), biological parameters (n = 10), sleep (n = 14), psychological comorbidities (n = 20), and hospitalization/health cost (n = 6). Sample sizes of the studies ranged from 75 (a prospective observational study) to 70,500 patients (a retrospective observational study) [20,30].

After 5 y follow up, CP group compared to no CP group had: Higher risk of all-cause mortality (HR = 1.05, 95%CI: 1.03–1.07), p < 0.05

Presence of CP increased all-cause mortality in the 24-month period by Kaplan–Meier’s analysis (log rank test, r2 = 4.698, p = 0.03) and cardiovascular mortality in this period (log rank test, r2 = 4.698, p = 0.03; after multivariate Cox proportional hazard regression: OR: 3.164; 95%CI: 1.743–5.744, p < 0.001); but did not increase infection related mortality.VAS: Not a significant factor to predict 24-month cardiovascular mortality, cardiovascular related mortality or infection related.

Mortality rate: 45.7% in the group suffering from CP vs. 54.9% in the group without CP.Patients with moderate to severe pruritus: Higher mortality rates (RR = 1.2; p < 0.02) compared with those with no/mild pruritus; no longer true after adjusting for age and sex.

sec.title: CKD-aP and physical component score (PCS) – seven articles
sec.p: PCS (physical component summary) is summary score derived from the SF-36 (Short Form Health Survey) or SF-12 which measures overall physical health.

PCS scores are typically standardized with a mean of 50 and a standard deviation of 10, based on the general population. Higher scores indicate better physical health, whereas lower scores reflect greater physical limitations.
Thus, lower PCS scores were found in patients with pruritus in all articles.

PCS decreased while pruritus increased when the lowest and highest pruritus severity scores were compared (7.15 points difference; p < 0.0001) [20]. Hemodialysis patients with moderate to extreme pruritus had a summary PCS score 3.27 points lower than those with no/mild pruritus (p < 0.001) [21].

Among maintenance hemodialysis (MHD) patients, those without pruritus had a mean score of 39.2 ± 11.0 points, whereas those who presented with pruritus had 6.7 points lower (95% confidence interval (95%CI): 6.1–7.2) [11].

Similar PCS scores were found in three other studies: 4.4 points lower (p < 0.0001), 3.3 points lower (p < 0.0001), and 6.4 points lower (p < 0.0001) in patients moderately to extremely bothered by pruritus, when compared with patients without pruritus [14,15,22]. A similar association was found in non-dialysis patients: the average PCS score was 7.6 points lower in patients with extreme pruritus compared to those without (95%CI: 29.8–25.4) [16].

sec.title: CKD-aP and comorbidities – 15 articles
sec.p: Severe pruritus in CKD patients was linked to worse prognosis compared to no, mild, or moderate pruritus (log-rank test, χ2 = 14.426; p = 0.0001) [23]. In non-dialysis CKD patients, pruritus independently contributed to CKD progression (odds ratio (OR) 1.15, 95%CI: 1.02–1.29; p = 0.02), along with anorexia and fatigue [24].

Six studies found an association between pruritus and cardiovascular comorbidities, five with a higher risk [11–13,25,26] and one found no association [17]. CKD-aP MHD patients had more cardiovascular comorbidities (values not detailed), diabetes (adjusted OR (aOR) = 1.41, 95%CI: 1.11–1.80), and psychiatric conditions (aOR = 1.66, 95%CI: 1.32–2.10) [12]. A higher cardiovascular disease risk in MHD patients with pruritus (χ2 = 4.649; p = 0.031) [25], and major adverse cardiac and cerebrovascular events (hazard ratio (HR): 1.05, 95%CI: 1.01–1.09) or heart failure (HR = 1.08, 95%CI: 1.01–1.16) were related in pruritic CKD patients [26]. Higher long-term morbidity risks, including infection-related hospitalization (HR = 1.13, 95%CI: 1.16–1.21), catheter-related infections (HR = 1.16, 95%CI: 1.12–1.21), and parathyroidectomy (HR = 1.10, 95%CI: 1.01–1.20) [26], as well as more cardiovascular events and skin infections, were reported in severe pruritus cases (HR = 1.29; 95%CI: 1.06–1.57 and HR = 1.41, 95%CI: 0.87–2.27, respectively) [11].

Pruritus was an independent risk factor for severe hypocalcemia post-parathyroidectomy (area under the curve (AUC) 0.591, 95%CI: 0.493–0.690), alongside preoperative hypocalcemia and high intact parathyroid hormone (iPTH) levels [27]. Osteoporosis was found in 87.5% of MHD CKD patients with pruritus, compared to 12.5% without (p = 0.03) [28].

Pruritus caused 50% of patients to skip or shorten HD sessions at least occasionally [29] whereas severe pruritus led to 2.6 more missed dialysis sessions per year [20]. However, Asghar et al. found no association between pruritus and frequency of dialysis [18].

Five studies found an association between pruritus and diabetes mellitus [12,13,17–19]. Studies conducted by Sukul et al. and Li et al. found that patients with more severe pruritus had a higher prevalence of diabetes mellitus (OR = 1.41, 95%CI: 1.11–1.80 for [12] and 34.8% for [19], p = 0.013). Conversely, Weisshaar et al. found that diabetes mellitus, with or without complications, was significantly less prevalent in patients with pruritus (p < 0.05) [17], while for Asghar et al. there was no significant association (OR = 1.688, 95%CI: 0.832–3.426, p = 0.148) [18].

sec.title: CKD-aP and mortality – 18 articles (Table 2)
sec.p: An association between pruritus and mortality was found in 15/18 studies [11,13,22,23,30–38] though the association was no longer significant after adjusting for sleep in 3/15 [14,15,21]. Two studies found no association [24,39].

Severe pruritus was identified as an independent mortality predictor (HR = 1.595; 95%CI: 1.160–2.381; p = 0.0084) [23]. Moderate pruritus increased mortality risk (OR = 3.2; 95%CI: 1.4–9.0; p = 0.027) [31]. A study of 25,916 CKD MHD patients found a higher adjusted HR for mortality in the pruritus group: HR = 1.13 (95%CI: 1.03–1.24) for moderate pruritus and HR = 1.30 (95%CI: 1.15–1.48) for severe pruritus [13].

In the Dialysis Outcomes and Practice Pattern Study (DOPPS) from 2009 to 2018, in which patients with two CKD-aP assessments 12 months apart with bothering itch were evaluated, they showed that those affected by pruritus at any point in the year (Y) had higher rates of all-cause mortality (HR = 1.22 (95%CI: 0.99–1.51) for patients with pruritus at Y1 but not Y2, HR = 1.32 (95%CI: 1.07–1.63) for that developing pruritus at Y2, and HR = 1.31 (95%CI: 1.11–1.56) for those with pruritus at Y1 and Y2) [36]. A higher mortality rate in patients with moderate to extreme pruritus across Japan DOPPS phases of data collection (1999–2001 phase 1, 2002–2004 phase 2, and 2005–2008 phase 3) was evidenced, with a significant increase in JDOPPS 3 (37%, p = 0.03; 22%, p = 0.04) after sleep quality adjustment [22]. Decreased survival in MHD patients with moderate/severe pruritus was confirmed (χ2 = 9.80; p = 0.0017) [32].

All-cause mortality (HR = 1.05; 95%CI: 1.03–1.07), infection-related death (HR = 1.08; 95%CI: 1.05–1.11), and cardiovascular death (HR = 1.06; 95%CI: 1.02–1.09) were increased in pruritic dialysis patients over five years [37], while a 20% higher cardiovascular and infection-related mortality in severe pruritus cases was found (HR = 1.29; 95%CI: 1.06–1.57; HR = 1.44; 95%CI: 1.05–1.96) [11]. MHD patients with pruritus had significantly higher all-cause and cardiovascular mortality at 24 months (log-rank test, r2 = 4.698, p = 0.03), with pruritus predicting 24-month cardiovascular mortality (OR = 3.164; 95%CI: 1.743–5.744, p < 0.001) [38].

The higher mortality risk (11%, p = 0.04 in [30] and 15%, p = 0.008 in [11]) in CKD patients with moderate to extreme pruritus, was not confirmed after sleep quality adjustment in two studies [14,21]. The 17% increased mortality risk in MHD patients with pruritus described in the different studies operated by the authors was no longer significant after sleep adjustment [15].

Some studies found an association between pruritus, mortality, and sleep. Severe to extreme pruritus was associated with extreme insomnia (aOR = 8.80; 95%CI: 3.24–23.91), and as extreme insomnia was associated with higher mortality, this suggested an association between pruritus and death (aOR = 1.72; 95%CI: 1.00–3.03; p = 0.05) [33]. Lower 20-month survival in patients with both pruritus and insomnia was noted (p = 0.037), without any significant difference for pruritus alone [34].
Additional associations include worse survival in patients with both moderate/severe pruritus and high hs-CRP (χ2 = 6.42, p = 0.011) [32].
CKD-aP PD patients with high VAS scores had a higher risk of technical failure or death (HR = 1.64; 95%CI: 1.18–2.28; p = 0.003) [35].

Anorexia was linked to CKD progression and death, but not significantly to pruritus [24]. In an observational study, the mortality rate was lower in the group with CKD-aP (45.7%) compared with the group without CKD-aP (54.9%) in MHD patients, without significance [39]. However, MHD patients with chronic secondary scratch lesions and chronic prurigo had a higher adjusted mortality risk compared to those without skin lesions [39].

sec.title: CKD-aP and psychological comorbidities – 20 articles (Table 3)
sec.p: A significant association was found in 18/20 [11,12,14–16,19–22,25,28,30,40–44,47] while two did not [45,46].

The seven trials investigating pruritus and the Mental Component Score (MCS) which is summary score derived from the SF-36 or SF-12 measuring mental health, showed a significant correlation between pruritus and lower MCS. In dialysis patients, pruritus negatively impacted quality of life. Thus, increased pruritus severity led to a decrease in MCS (52.07–43.46; p < 0.0001) and worsened daily life, disease burden, and symptom scores [20]. MHD patients with severe pruritus had MCS scores 9.1 points lower than those without (95%CI: 8.4–9.8). They were also more likely to experience depressive symptoms, exhaustion, dizziness, and hospitalizations for mental status changes (HR = 1.84; 95%CI: 1.18–2.87 for hospitalizations) [11]. DOPPS-based studies revealed that MCS scores were lower by 3.77, 4.3, and 8.6 points in MHD patients with moderate to extreme pruritus (p < 0.0001) [14,15,21]. These patients were also 2.3–5.2 times more likely to feel exhausted (p < 0.0001) and 1.3–1.7 times more likely to have depressive symptoms (p < 0.001) [14,15]. MHD patients with pruritus had higher risk of being diagnosed with depression (aOR = 1.4, p < 0.001) [21]. In non-dialysis CKD patients, MCS declined if pruritus severity increased (−6.2 points, 95%CI: −8.5 to −3.9). Patients with extreme pruritus had a 2.5 times higher likelihood of depression (prevalence ratio (PR) = 2.55; 95%CI: 2.07–3.13) [16].

In the Japanese study JDOPPS, Kimata et al. showed that MHD patients with pruritus had lower MCS scores (−4.6 points, p < 0.0001) and were 2.2–5.8 times more likely to feel exhausted (p < 0.0001) [22].

A strong association between severe pruritus and depression (OR = 15.971; 95%CI: 5.173–49.315; p < 0.001) and anxiety was observed, with an increase with pruritus severity [35]. In a prospective study in 2016, Sukul et al. showed that pruritus is more common in patients with psychiatric disorders, although no real correlation could be established (aOR = 1.66; 95%CI: 1.32–2.10) [12]. However, visual analogue scale (VAS) and the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) in MHD patients with pruritus were correlated (r2 = 0.184, p = 0.034) [25].

Depressive symptoms in MHD patients correlated with pruritus severity (5-D scores) and were linked to higher PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) (p = 0.001) and DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index) scores (p = 0.005) [41]. MHD patients with severe pruritus treated with PD had higher BDI (Beck Depression Inventory) scores (OR = 1.429; 95%CI: 1.05–1.52; p = 0.002) [19]. Afsar and Elsurer also described the significant association between VAS pruritus score and BDI score (95%CI: 0.060–0.0721, p = 0.025) [30]. Two cross-sectional studies confirmed independent associations between pruritus and depression (OR = 2.011; 95%CI: 0.043–3.81; p = 0.045 for [42], p = 0.001 for [43]).
Dialysis patients with pruritus experienced mood changes, depression, and sexual dysfunction [47]. ItchyQoL and HADS anxiety scores were worsened in patients with persistent pruritus (p < 0.05) [28].
Two studies found no association between pruritus and suicidal ideation in one (p = 0.595) [45] no impact of pruritus on emotional well-being in CKD patients in the other (p = 0.316) [46].

sec.title: CKD-aP and sleep – 15 articles (Table 4)
sec.p: A total of 14/15 studies found a relationship between pruritus and sleep [11,14–16,21–23,28,33,36,44,45,47], but one did not [46].

Pruritus affected sleep quality in dialysis patients [44]. Patients with non-persistent CKD-aP had significantly less sleep disturbance than those with persistent pruritus (p < 0.04) [28]. Sukul et al. described that 32.1% of the 22,464 patients included in their observational study were poor sleepers [11]. This rate rose more than 70% of MHD patients with severe pruritus experiencing sleep disturbances in a longitudinal study [23]. Similar findings showed an increased risk of poor sleep quality in MHD patients with moderate to severe pruritus, with risks 1.9–3.7 times higher (p < 0.0001), 1.5–4.1 times higher, and 1.4–4.0 times higher (p > 0.001) [14,15,22]. Similar results were given in a retrospective study (aOR = 1.24–2.17, p < 0.001) [21].

The association between pruritus and sleep deprivation in dialysis patients was confirmed (30.9% vs. 4.3%, p < 0.001) [45]. Poor sleep quality included waking at night, insufficient sleep, and daytime sleepiness. MHD patients with severe pruritus had more severe insomnia (aOR = 8.8; 95%CI: 3.24–23.91) [33]. A strong association between sleep disturbance and pruritus intensity in MHD patients was shown (p < 0.0001), while PD patients with severe pruritus had worse Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) scores than those without pruritus (p < 0.001) [19,47].

In a two-year longitudinal study, MHD patients who developed pruritus in the second year had an increase in sleep disturbances (38–48%), while those who no longer had pruritus saw a decrease (48–40%). Sleep disturbance rates remained stable if pruritus status did not change [36].
Among non-dialysis CKD patients, those with pruritus had significantly more restless sleep: PR of 1.69 (95%CI: 1.49–1.91) for moderate pruritus and 2.10 (95%CI: 1.76–2.51) for extreme pruritus [16].
The absence of association between pruritus and sleep quality in CKD patients was found in an only study [46].

sec.title: CKD-aP and biological markers – 11 articles (Table 5)
sec.p: A retrospective study of CKD and HD patients found that those with pruritus had worse biological parameters, including higher serum urea, creatinine, serum phosphorus, and iPTH levels (p < 0.05), as well as lower blood calcium levels compared to CKD patients without pruritus (p < 0.05). Pruritus intensity correlated with all these parameters except serum calcium [46].

Albumin <3.5 g/dL (aOR = 2.987; 95%CI: 1.156–7.716; p = 0.024), phosphate >4.5 mg/dL (aOR = 3.889; 95%CI: 1.118–15.532; p = 0.033), and potassium >5.1 mEq/L (aOR = 2.934; 95%CI: 1.030–8.355; p = 0.044) were significantly associated with pruritus in MHD patients. However, no association was found with hyperparathyroidism, hypocalcemia, creatinine, urea, or dialysis frequency [18].

Afsar and Elsurer showed that CKD patients with pruritus had higher calcium–phosphorus ratios (+0.624, 95%CI: 0.041–0.092, p < 0.0001), iPTH levels (+0.389, 95%CI: 0.002–0.006, p < 0.0001), and glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) levels (+0.285, 95%CI: 0.024–0.547, p = 0.033) [30]. They also demonstrated that VAS score was significantly correlated with blood glucose (r = +0.271, p = 0.019), serum calcium (r = +0.534, p < 0.0001), serum phosphorus (r = +0.521, p < 0.0001), calcium–phosphate product (r = +0.609, p < 0.0001), iPTH (r = +0.422, p < 0.0001) and HbA1c (r = +0.602, p < 0.0001) [30].

Higher iPTH level was statistically associated with pruritus in a cross-sectional study of CKD patients undergoing PD with severe pruritus (OR = 1.317; 95%CI: 1.10–1.83, p = 0.014) [19]. Weisshaar et al. found higher PTH levels in MHD patients with pruritus (376 ng/L ± 380.2 vs. 289.9 ng/L ± 392.4, p < 0.004), along with significantly higher creatinine (8.9 mg/dL vs. 7.8 mg/dL) and phosphorus (1.0 mmol/L ± 0.6 vs. 1.7 mmol/L ± 0.5; p < 0.004 for both) [17]. However, no association was found between pruritus and anemia, hyperparathyroidism, or dialysis membrane type.

An observational study linked higher serum urea to the presence and intensity of pruritus in 131 MHD patients (p < 0.01 for both) and also to lower vitamin B12 levels (p < 0.01 and p < 0.05, respectively) [47].

Higher levels of hs-CRP and lower levels of iPTH (p = 0.038) and albumin (p = 0.036) were observed in MHD patients with moderate/severe pruritus compared to those with no/mild pruritus [32]. Lower albumin levels were also noted in patients with severe pruritus in four studies, significant in three [13,18,32], not significant for the other [11].

Severe to extreme pruritus was independently and significantly associated with hyperphosphatemia (aOR = 1.34; 95%CI: 1.06–1.70) and anemia (aOR = 1.27; 95%CI: 1.04–1.56) in an observational study of CKD patients undergoing HD [33].
Higher white blood cell count was significantly associated with pruritus in MHD patients (p = 0.049) [25].

sec.title: CKD-aP and hospitalization/health cost – six articles (Table 6)
sec.p: The six articles that examined the relationship between pruritus and healthcare costs found an increase in the latter in case of pruritus [11,20,23,26,47,48].

A higher rate of hospitalization was observed in HD patients with severe pruritus compared to those without pruritus (42.5% vs. 28.4%), resulting in higher total healthcare costs for patients with severe pruritus ($33,755 vs. $26,933 for itch; $29,801 vs. $26,321 for dryness; and $29,249 vs. $26,736 for combined itch and dryness) [48]. MHD patients with severe pruritus were more likely to be hospitalized for psychiatric conditions such as altered mental status or confusion (HR = 1.84; 95%CI: 1.18–2.87) [11].

Four studies have highlighted the increased use of medication in MHD patients with pruritus. Thus, the intensity of pruritus was strongly correlated with the use of any type of antipruritic treatment (p < 0.001 for the use of antihistamines, antipruritic lotions, and sedatives), between the three groups of pruritus (mild, moderate, and severe) and between severe pruritus and the others [23]. The subgroup of MHD patients with mild pruritus used erythropoietin (EPO) more frequently than the other groups (p = 0.0105) [23]. In an observational prospective study on 70,500 patients, Ramakrishnan et al. described higher use of EPO doses (55,653.03 vs. 66,212.51 EPO units, p < 0.0001) and intravenous iron injections (257.84 units vs. 262.55 units, p < 0.0001) in patients with severe pruritus [20]. The use of antibiotics increased with the severity of pruritus (4.1% in patients with no pruritus to 20.7% in patients with severe pruritus, p < 0.05) [20]. MHD patients with moderate to extreme pruritus were 2.1 times more likely to use antihistamines, 1.5 times more likely to use hypnotics, 1.2 times more likely to use benzodiazepines, and 1.5 times more likely to use antidepressants (p < 0.001) [26]. The higher use of antidepressants in MHD patients with pruritus (p < 0.05) compared to those without pruritus was also reported [47].

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The primary objective of this study was to perform a meta-analysis. However, a minimum of five studies using comparable statistical methods, reporting the same outcomes, and involving similar populations is required to achieve a sufficiently powered analysis. Due to substantial heterogeneity in study design (prospective, retrospective, cross-sectional), data availability, and outcome assessment tools – particularly those used for pruritus (VAS, KDQOL, CKDopps, etc.) a formal meta-analysis was not feasible. Consequently, we conducted a narrative systematic review.

Our review of 37 studies consistently demonstrated that pruritus in CKD patients is associated with higher mortality, cardiovascular events, and increased healthcare costs. Beyond its psychosocial impact and reduction in quality of life, pruritus contributes to comorbidity, disease progression, and mortality, as highlighted by Molina et al. [49]. Specifically, pruritus was found to be linked with altered biological markers, higher risk of depression, sleep disturbance, increased healthcare utilization, and poorer survival outcomes.

Several studies also reported a significant association between pruritus and cardiovascular events. This relationship has been hypothesized to arise from systemic inflammation, as suggested by elevated hs-CRP levels, lower serum albumin and higher WBC counts in affected patients. CRP is considered to be the most reliable inflammatory marker in HD patients, it is quite surprising that its assay was only reported in one study [32].

Biological correlates of pruritus in CKD included elevated iPTH, creatinine, phosphorus, and urea in most studies, although some conflicting findings emerged. For example, one study reported lower iPTH levels [32], while others observed increased levels. The authors explain that high CRP reflects chronic inflammation, which could lead to metabolic disorders and/or parathyroid dysfunction, causing this decrease in iPTH. Furthermore, reduced calcium or albumin associated with iPTH variation may explain the higher frequency of osteoporosis in pruritic CKD patients [28]. These associations were confirmed in both non-dialysis CKD and HD populations [48,49]. Additional comorbidities, including infections and diabetes, were also more prevalent among patients with pruritus in both CKD and liver disease. However, for diabetes mellitus, despite the overall consistency of results, two studies reported conflicting findings. For instance, Weisshaar et al. did not confirm significant differences between patients with and without pruritus in congestive heart failure and peripheral vascular disease, and even reported fewer cases of diabetes in the CKD-aP group, contrary to most other studies [17]. Since pruritus is frequently reported in diabetes mellitus independently of CKD [50], these discrepancies may reflect small cohort sizes and study-specific limitations. Similarly, Asghar et al. [18] failed to demonstrate an association between pruritus and diabetes, likely due to insufficient statistical power.

Survival outcomes were extensively investigated: 16 studies assessed mortality in renal failure, and all but one found a positive association with pruritus. However, this association was attenuated after adjusting for sleep disorders in five studies, suggesting that insomnia may partially mediate the observed mortality risk. More broadly, inflammation and comorbidities appear to be key mediators, explaining the increased all-cause and cardiovascular mortality in pruritic patients. Reinforcing this evidence, two additional studies published after the inclusion period also confirmed an association between pruritus, mortality, cardiovascular events, and hospitalizations [51,52]. Furthermore, two studies linked pruritus to missed HD sessions, a factor independently associated with increased mortality [17,29].

Pruritus also has a profound psychosocial impact. In CKD patients, it is consistently associated with depression, anxiety, sleep disturbance, and impaired sexual function [44]. These complications not only worsen patients’ daily functioning but also contribute indirectly to increased morbidity and mortality. Additionally, they drive healthcare utilization: several studies showed nearly doubled healthcare costs per patient, due to higher medication use, longer hospital stays, and more psychiatric admissions. This finding was confirmed by an observational study published in June 2023, which demonstrated an association between pruritus, increased drug prescriptions, and higher hospitalization rates [53].

Based on the data from the various articles, we can put forward a ‘Strength of evidence’ for the various items described, summarized in Table 7. Across CKD populations, pruritus consistently appears as a clinically meaningful indicator rather than an isolated symptom. Several domains show robust and convergent evidence relationships. Concerning mortality, moderate to severe pruritus is associated with higher all-cause and cardiovascular mortality, with HRs around 1.1–1.9. The association often weakens after adjustment for sleep, suggesting partial mediation by sleep disturbance. Still, severe pruritus repeatedly emerges as a risk marker. Concerning comorbidities, patients with more severe pruritus tend to have a greater comorbidity burden, including more diabetes, cardiovascular disease, liver disease, and infections, as well as lower PCS scores. Although some comorbidities show variability across cohorts, the overall pattern suggests a more complex clinical profile in pruritic patients. About biological markers, repeated associations link pruritus with mineral metabolism disorders (increased phosphate, calcium–phosphorus, and iPTH), inflammation (hs-CRP increased), and poorer nutritional status (decrease of albumin and hemoglobin). Several abnormalities remain independent predictors, supporting a biologically plausible aspect. Concerning mental health and QoL, evidence is particularly strong for psychological impact. Pruritus severity correlates with higher rates of depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion, and significantly lower MCS. Dose–response patterns are observed across multiple validated scales, and pruritus frequently acts as an independent predictor. Sleep impairment shows the strongest and most consistent association. Moderate to severe pruritus increases insomnia and nocturnal awakenings by 1.4–4 times, with severe forms affecting >70% of patients. Pruritus remains an independent determinant of poor sleep, reinforcing its clinical significance. Finally, concerning health costs, pruritic patients experience more hospitalizations, especially for confusion or infections, and require more medications (antihistamines, hypnotics, benzodiazepines, and antidepressants). Higher treatment burden translates into increased healthcare costs. Overall, together, these findings show that pruritus reflects a broader burden of disease in CKD – biological, clinical, psychological, and economic. Its systematic assessment can help nephrologists identify patients at higher risk and guide more comprehensive management.

sec.p: Several biases, found in clinical trials, are also present in these studied publications. First, heterogeneity in study design, populations, and assessment methods complicates comparisons and induces a detection bias. Studies varied in timing of pruritus assessment, which was sometimes performed only at baseline and not longitudinally, preventing causal inference [11,20]. Moreover, variability in measurement methods of pruritus (VAS, KDQOL, ItchyQoL, CKDopps, etc.) further hampers comparability.

Selection bias was also present, with many studies being single-center and involving small sample sizes. Of the 37 studies, 27 concerned HD patients, while only two investigated PD populations, and six did not distinguish between dialysis modalities. Although CKD-aP occurs in both PD and HD patients, prevalence estimates remain contradictory [54,55] limiting generalizability.
Patient attrition due to mortality may have introduced survival bias, as those with more severe pruritus and comorbidities may have been underrepresented in follow-up analyses [14,22,34].

Another limitation relates to the assessment of pruritus itself, which was predominantly self-reported. This approach is prone to recall and misclassification biases, especially when nonspecific quality-of-life tools rather than pruritus-specific scales were used.

Publication bias may also have influenced the available evidence, as studies with negative results are less likely to be published. We also acknowledge that the inclusion of abstracts may be criticized; however, we selected only those in which the association between pruritus and the outcome was explicitly quantified, while acknowledging that some relevant studies may have been missed due to search term limitations.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Despite these limitations, the convergence of findings across multiple studies in diverse settings supports the robustness of the observed associations. Taken together, the evidence strongly suggests that pruritus in CKD is not a benign symptom but a clinically relevant factor contributing to poorer quality of life, increased comorbidities, higher morbidity, increased healthcare utilization, and excess mortality. Dialysis patients may benefit from targeted treatments, including DFK.
These findings highlight the need for nephrologists and dermatologists to assess new therapies for pruritus and its broader health effects.

ack.p: JS conceived, collected the data, realized the analysis, and wrote the manuscript; EB and ASF helped with the interpretation and the writing of the manuscript; GR and LM reviewed and edited the manuscript; CLGI helped with the analysis, the project administration and the writing of the original manuscript and the reviewed versions.

sec.title: Ethics statement
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: JS, ASF, EB, and CLGI have no conflicts of interest to declare. LM and GR declare conflict with Vifor.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this article. Further enquiries can be directed to the corresponding author.
